topic: A person's longtime hobby becomes their child's obsession
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias watched his daughter, Clara, with a tightening in his chest. For twenty years, woodworking had been his sanctuary—the scent of sawdust and linseed oil a comforting balm to his soul. Now, that sanctuary felt invaded. Clara, barely ten, had traded her carefully sharpened pencils for miniature chisels and a relentless focus on replicating the intricate dovetail joints he used to perfect. Every time he walked into the workshop, the air would hum with the high-pitched whine of a hand plane, and Clara would be hunched over a piece of cherry wood, her brow furrowed in intense concentration. He picked up his own plane, the familiar weight suddenly feeling alien in his hands. He found himself fiddling with the handle, his gaze darting from the smooth curve of the wood to the precision of her tiny movements. "That's a beautiful curve, sweetheart," he managed, his voice sounding thin. Clara didn't look up, her lips moving silently as she sanded a joint with microscopic care. The quiet satisfaction he once found in creation was replaced by a hollow ache.

[story 2]

I remember the afternoon I found the new set of Japanese carving tools. They were exquisite, the steel gleaming under the garage light. I’d spent years perfecting the technique of relief carving on maple, a meditative process that demanded absolute stillness. Then Clara showed up, not asking for help, but demanding access. She didn't want to just look; she wanted to *do*. She’d dragged out the old workbench, covered in my old projects, and started experimenting with gouges and V-tools. Her enthusiasm was infectious, almost overwhelming. When she finally managed to carve a surprisingly delicate bird, the way she beamed—a wide, triumphant smile—it struck me. My own hands felt clumsy, heavy. I retreated to the corner, ostensibly to organize some lumber, but really just to stand back and observe the relentless, vibrant energy she poured into the material. I felt a strange tightness around my jaw, a dull pressure behind my eyes that made me want to retreat entirely.

[story 3]

The sound of scraping metal was the soundtrack to my evenings. It used to be a peaceful rhythm, the steady scrape of my own tools against oak, a language I understood perfectly. Now, it was Clara’s symphony. She’d brought home a collection of miniature brass fittings and began constructing elaborate, tiny mechanical figures. She treated the hobby with a seriousness that bordered on obsession, meticulously cataloging her findings and planning complex assemblies. I watched from the doorway, leaning against the frame, trying to appear casual while every nerve ending screamed at the disparity. When she finally presented me with a tiny, functional gear mechanism, perfectly balanced and gleaming, she held it up as if presenting a masterpiece. Her eyes, bright with accomplishment, sought mine. I offered a weak smile, my throat suddenly dry. "Wow, Clara. That's... incredible." The praise felt insufficient, a mere acknowledgment of a passion that had entirely eclipsed mine.
